text: This is a presentation of the laparoscopic management of a large iatrogenic duodenal perforation. I have no disclosures. This patient is a sixty-nine year old female undergoing evaluation by gastroenterology for pancreatitis. She was scheduled for an endoscopic ultrasound. Her past medical history is significant for a medically managed coronary artery disease.

The laparoscopic view shows the port placement for diagnostic laparoscopy. Upon entry, free fluid is visible in the abdominal cavity, along with adhesions in the gallbladder fossa, but no gross spillage is observed.

Two additional trocars are placed in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen for instrument access. The liver is retracted to reveal a large, clean-edged, full-thickness transverse perforation at the anterior aspect of the second portion of the duodenum. The surgeon prepares for primary repair of the defect.

The surgeon performs a running suture repair on the duodenal perforation, carefully taking full thickness bites to avoid narrowing the duodenal lumen. Hemostasis is achieved as the repair site is irrigated, resulting in a final repair measuring five centimeters in length.

The surgeon uses a silk suture to perform a series of interrupted Lembert stitches, ensuring the edges of the incision are well imbricated. The incision is examined, with one out of every three sutures left intentionally long. A piece of omentum is mobilized and placed over the repair as a patch.

The surgeon uses the tails of previously repaired stitches to secure an omentum patch over the duodenal repair. The tails of the sutures are trimmed, followed by copious irrigation to wash out any spillage in both upper quadrants. A vibrant sealant is applied to reinforce the repair around the omentum patch.

A flat Jackson-Pratt drain is placed in the surgical field. An endoscopy is performed to visually confirm the continuity and patency of the duodenum, with interluminal evaluation of the repaired area to ensure no stricture formation. A percutaneous gastrostomy tube is inserted for drainage.

Patient did well postoperatively and was discharged home on postoperative day four on a soft diet. Subsequently, she had an upper GI contrast study done at one month follow up. Here we can see the contrast flowing freely from the stomach into the small bowel.
